glossary.definition: the conditions that determine when the K-Means algorithm should stop iterating.
glossary.sentence: The typical termination criteria of the clustering process in the K-Means algorithm is an iteration when data objects do not change their cluster membership (centroid values do not change).

glossary.definition: a method of hierarchical clustering that starts with each data object in its own cluster and then recursively merges the two clusters with the smallest intergroup dissimilarity until one single cluster is formed.
glossary.sentence: Figure 2 represents the process of agglomerative hierarchical clustering in which clusters having the smallest distance between them are merged at each step of clustering.

glossary.definition: a method of hierarchical clustering that starts with all data objects in a single cluster and then recursively splits clusters into two new clusters based on the largest between-group dissimilarity until each data object forms its own cluster.
glossary.sentence: Divisive hierarchical clustering applies a top-down clustering approach.

glossary.definition: a tree-like diagram representing the hierarchical relationships between data objects in a dataset, where clades indicate merging of data objects into clusters and their height indicates cluster similarity.
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram is an output of hierarchical clustering.

glossary.definition: a part of a dendrogram representing the merging of data objects into clusters, with its height indicating the similarity between the merged data objects.
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram consists of (Figure 5): clades that represent merging of data objects into clusters;

glossary.definition: methods used to measure the similarity or distance between clusters, which determine how clusters are merged during hierarchical clustering.
glossary.sentence: There are several methods to measure the similarity (distance) between clusters to decide the rules for merging clusters, and they are often called linkage methods .

glossary.definition: a linkage method in hierarchical clustering where the distance between two clusters is calculated as the shortest distance between any pair of data objects from each cluster.
glossary.sentence: Single-linkage : in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the closest elements of the two clusters (the shortest distance) is calculated:

glossary.definition: a linkage method in hierarchical clustering where the distance between two clusters is calculated as the longest distance between any pair of data objects from each cluster.
glossary.sentence: Complete-linkage : in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the clusters’ most distant elements (the longest distance) is calculated:

glossary.definition: a linkage method in hierarchical clustering where the distance between two clusters is defined as the average distance between each data object in one cluster to every data object in the other cluster.
glossary.sentence: Average-linkage: the distance between two clusters is defined as the average distance between each data object in one cluster to every data object in the other cluster:

glossary.definition: the measure of how different two groups (clusters) are from each other, used to determine which clusters to merge during hierarchical clustering.
glossary.sentence: The pairs chosen for merging consist of the two groups with the smallest intergroup dissimilarity (the fragment is based on the information given in (Hastie, 2017)) .

glossary.definition: the measure of how different two groups (clusters) are from each other, used to determine which clusters to split during divisive hierarchical clustering.
glossary.sentence: The split is chosen to produce two new groups with the largest between-group dissimilarity (the fragment is based on the information given in (Hastie, 2017)) .

glossary.definition: a horizontal line drawn through a dendrogram to determine the number of clusters by counting how many vertical lines are intersected by the cut-off line.
glossary.sentence: A horizontal cut- off line is usually made through the dendrogram to decide the number of clusters based on the hierarchical clustering algorithm.

glossary.definition: a horizontal line drawn through a dendrogram to determine the number of clusters by counting how many vertical lines are intersected by the cut-off line.
glossary.sentence: A horizontal cut- off line is usually made through the dendrogram to decide the number of clusters based on the hierarchical clustering algorithm.

glossary.definition: lines in a dendrogram representing individual data objects, intersected by horizontal cut-off lines to determine the number of clusters.
glossary.sentence: The number of vertical lines intersected by the horizontal cut- off line represents the number of clusters.

glossary.definition: a group of data objects that are similar to each other and have been merged or split during the hierarchical clustering process.
glossary.sentence: Each data object initially is attributed to its own cluster.

glossary.definition: the process of combining two clusters into a single larger cluster during agglomerative hierarchical clustering.
glossary.sentence: Then, at each level, a selected pair of clusters is recursively merged into a single cluster.

glossary.definition: the process of dividing one cluster into two new sub-clusters during divisive hierarchical clustering.
glossary.sentence: Then, at each hierarchy level, it recursively splits one of the existing clusters into two new clusters.

glossary.definition: the structure formed by the sequential merging or splitting of clusters in hierarchical clustering, represented as a tree-like diagram (dendrogram).
glossary.sentence: The steps of the agglomerative hierarchical clustering can be described as follows (Figure 3): attribute each data object to a single cluster.

glossary.definition: a method of processing that starts with the entire dataset and splits it into smaller clusters, used in divisive hierarchical clustering.
glossary.sentence: Divisive hierarchical clustering applies a top-down clustering approach.

glossary.definition: a method of processing that starts with individual data objects and merges them into larger clusters, used in agglomerative hierarchical clustering.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, each data object initially is attributed to its own cluster.

glossary.definition: a measure of how different two data objects or clusters are from each other, used to determine merging or splitting during hierarchical clustering.
glossary.sentence: The pairs chosen for merging consist of the two groups with the smallest intergroup dissimilarity (the fragment is based on the information given in (Hastie, 2017)) .

glossary.definition: a measure of how similar two data objects or clusters are to each other, used in hierarchical clustering to determine merging rules.
glossary.sentence: There are several methods to measure the similarity (distance) between clusters to decide the rules for merging clusters, and they are often called linkage methods .

glossary.definition: measures used to compare the performance of different algorithms or analyze the behavior of a single algorithm by tuning its hyperparameters.
glossary.sentence: Metrics for evaluating the machine learning model performance are used to: compare the performance of the different algorithms used on the same dataset and/or analyse the behaviour of the same algorithm by tuning values of its hyperparameters.

glossary.definition: A metric that measures the average of the squared prediction errors for individual data objects, providing positive non-zero values and having units of measurement as squared units of the quantity being estimated.
glossary.sentence: The Mean squared error (MSE) measures the average of the squared prediction errors for individual data objects. It provides positive non-zero values. It is calculated in the following way: This metric has the same units of measurement as the quantity being estimated. Still, they are squared units; for example, if the classifier predicts the number of graduates, the error will represent "the squared number of persons"; if it predicts the house prices in EUR, then the error value will represent "squared EUR".

glossary.definition: A metric that applies a square root to MSE, providing an error measure in the same unit as the target variable. It is calculated in the following way: The smaller the value, the better the predictive accuracy of the model is.
glossary.sentence: Root mean square error (RMSE) applies a square root to MSE and therefore provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable, for example, if the classifier predicts the number of graduates, then the error will represent "the number of persons"; if it predicts the prices of the house in EUR, then the error value will represent "price in EUR". The smaller the value, the better the predictive accuracy of the model is.

glossary.definition: A metric that calculates the average of the sums of the absolute difference between predicted output values and actual output values. It provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable.
glossary.sentence: Mean absolute error (MAE) is the average of the sums of the absolute difference between predicted output values and actual output values. It also provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable. It is calculated in the following way:

glossary.definition: A metric known as the coefficient of determination that indicates how close the model built (the line/plane found by the regression algorithm) fits the actual data objects available in the dataset. Its values range from 0 to 1, where 0 indicates no fit and 1 indicates a perfect fit.
glossary.sentence: R2 coefficient is also known as the coefficient of determination. It indicates how close the model built (the line/plane found by the regression algorithm) fits the actual data objects available in the dataset. The coefficient values lie in a range [0,1], where 0 indicates that the model does not fit the data objects and 1 indicates the perfect fit.

glossary.definition: Correct predictions where a data object is positive (label 1), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 1). For example, an e-mail is classified as spam, and it is spam.
glossary.sentence: Correct predictions: True positives (TP) – a data object is positive (label 1), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 1). For example, an e-mail is classified as spam, and it is spam.

glossary.definition: Correct predictions where a data object is negative (label 0), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 0). For example, an e-mail is classified as non-spam, and it is non-spam.
glossary.sentence: True negatives (TN) - a data object is negative (label 0), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 0). For example, an e-mail is classified as non-spam, and it is non-spam.

glossary.definition: Erroneous predictions where a data object is negative (label 0), but the algorithm labels it as positive (label 1). For example, an e-mail is classified as spam, but it is not.
glossary.sentence: False positives (FP) – a data object is negative (label 0), but the algorithm labels it as positive (label 1); for example, an e-mail is classified as spam, but it is not. In this case, we can say that a data object is "falsely" predicted as positive.

glossary.definition: Erroneous predictions where a data object is positive (label 1), but the classifier labels it as negative (label 0). For example, an e-mail is classified as non-spam, but it is spam.
glossary.sentence: False negatives (FN) – a data object is positive (label 1), but the classifier labels it as negative (label 0), for example, an e-mail is classified as non-spam, but it is spam. In this case, we can say that a data object is "falsely" predicted as negative.

glossary.definition: A table that summarises the performance of a classification algorithm by showing true positives, false positives, true negatives, and false negatives.
glossary.sentence: In the multiclass classification task, we first calculate a precision for each class, taking the value on the diagonal as true positives and the sum of other values in the column corresponding to the class as false positives.

glossary.definition: A metric that evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects. It is calculated in the following way (Kubat, 2017) : Accuracy for the multiclass classification is calculated by summing up values of the main diagonal of the confusion matrix and dividing it by the total number of data objects in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: Accuracy metric evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects. It is calculated in the following way (Kubat, 2017) : Accuracy for the multiclass classification is calculated by summing up values of the main diagonal of the confusion matrix and dividing it by the total number of data objects in the dataset.

glossary.definition: A performance metric which measures the proportion of correct predictions (both true positives and true negatives) among all predicted cases.
glossary.sentence: In this case, a poorly built “dummy” model which always predicts Class 0, achieves a 95% Accuracy, which indicates a very strong model.

glossary.definition: A category to which data objects are assigned in a classification task.
glossary.sentence: 95 of which labelled as belonging to Class 0, and 5 labelled as Class 1.

glossary.definition: A case where the actual class is positive but the model predicts it as negative.
glossary.sentence: In the multiclass classification, we first calculate a recall for each specific class, taking the value on the diagonal as true positives and the sum of other values in the row corresponding to the class as false negatives.

glossary.definition: A case where the actual class is negative but the model predicts it as positive.
glossary.sentence: In the multiclass classification task, we first calculate a precision for each class, taking the value on the diagonal as true positives and the sum of other values in the column corresponding to the class as false positives.

glossary.definition: A method of averaging metrics by calculating the average of each metric separately for all classes and then taking the mean of those averages.
glossary.sentence: After that, we calculate the macro average precision for the task by summing up precisions for all classes and dividing the sum by the number of classes.

glossary.definition: A metric that evaluates a fraction of predicted positives that are true positives. It is calculated as the ratio of true positives to the sum of true positives and false positives.
glossary.sentence: Precision is the probability that the classifier is right when labelling an example as positive (Kubat, 2017).

glossary.definition: A metric that evaluates a fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects. It is calculated as the ratio of true positives to the sum of true positives and false negatives.
glossary.sentence: Recall is the probability that a positive data object will be correctly recognised by the algorithm (Kubat, 2017).

glossary.definition: A case where the actual class is negative and the model predicts it as negative.
glossary.sentence: In the multiclass classification task, we first calculate a precision for each class, taking the value on the diagonal as true positives and the sum of other values in the column corresponding to the class as false positives.

glossary.definition: A case where the actual class is positive and the model predicts it as positive.
glossary.sentence: In the mult, we first calculate a precision for each class, taking the value on the diagonal as true positives and the sum of other values in the column corresponding to the class as false positives.

glossary.definition: A type of machine learning where the algorithm is not provided with any labels, and the goal is to find patterns or structures in the data without prior information on which group each instance belongs to.
glossary.sentence: It is a task of unsupervised machine learning, there are no labelling of data objects.

glossary.definition: A plot where the cluster label is on the y-axis and the Silhouette Score is on the x-axis, typically used to visualize the quality of clustering.
glossary.sentence: The Silhouette Scores are usually plotted on the so-called Silhouette Plot , representing the cluster label on the y-axis and the Silhouette Score on the x-axis (Fig.6).

glossary.definition: A mathematical function applied to the weighted sum of inputs by a neuron to produce its output. It is used in both hidden and output layers.
glossary.sentence: Each processing unit calculates the weighted sum, including the bias input, and then applies the activation function.

glossary.definition: A type of neural network that has only one processing layer; the input layer does not contain computing neurons and thus does not make processing, while the output layer contains computing neurons and provides the final output of the network. All inputs are connected directly to the outputs.
glossary.sentence: In a single layer perceptron (Hopgood, 2001; Jones, 2009; Russell & Norvig, 2010): there is an input layer that does not contain computing neurons and thus does not make processing; there is an output layer containing the computing neurons and providing the final output of the network.

glossary.definition: A neural network that learns the best possible values for weights to correctly classify data. It adjusts its weights based on small changes after each training example to reduce the difference between actual and desired output.
glossary.sentence: The perceptron learns the best possible values for weights that will allow correctly to classify the data (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: One cycle in which the artificial neural network uses all of the data objects contained in the training dataset exactly once for learning purposes. It corresponds to the number of times that the network will work through the entire training dataset.
glossary.sentence: An epoch represents one cycle in which the artificial neural network uses all of the data objects contained in the training dataset exactly once for learning purposes.

glossary.definition: A method used to compute weight correction by applying it to adjust the weights of the perceptron based on the difference between actual and desired output.
glossary.sentence: The weight correction is usually computed by applying the delta rule : Therefore, the complete rule for adjustments of the weights of the perceptron is the following one:

glossary.definition: A basic type of feed-forward neural network that can represent any continuous function with one hidden layer and even discontinuous functions with two hidden layers. It has an input layer, at least one hidden layer, and an output layer.
glossary.sentence: The multilayer perceptron is a basic type of feed-forward neural network that, with one hidden layer, can represent any continuous function of the input signals and with two hidden layers - even discontinuous functions ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A type of neural network where information flows in one direction, from input to output through hidden layers. It has greater processing power and can model practically any function of arbitrary complexity.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, a feed-forward neural network has greater processing power, and it can model practically any function of arbitrary complexity, where the number of inputs and the number of hidden layers determine the function complexity (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: A layer in a neural network that accepts input signals from the environment and sends these signals to hidden layers. It does not include computing neurons.
glossary.sentence: The input layer is pre-defined by the number of input features. The input layer that accepts input signals from the environment and sends these signals to hidden layers. It does not include computing neurons;

glossary.definition: A layer in a neural network that sends the numerical output produced by the neural network to the environment and consists of computing neurons.
glossary.sentence: The output layer is also pre-defined because the number of its processing units corresponds to the number of classification categories/classes. The output layer that sends the numerical output produced by the neural network to the environment and consists of computing neurons;

glossary.definition: A layer in a neural network that consists of computing neurons and usually performs the detection of features. Neurons in the hidden layer cannot be observed through the input/output behaviour, and there is no obvious way to know what the desired output of the hidden layer should be.
glossary.sentence: at least one hidden layer that consists of computing neurons and usually performs the detection of features. The hidden layer that consists of computing neurons and usually performs the detection of features; Neurons in the hidden layer cannot be observed through the input/output behaviour, and there is no obvious way to know what the desired output of the hidden layer should be.

glossary.definition: An additional input added to each neuron in a neural network to allow for more flexible modeling. It is included in the weighted sum calculation for each processing unit.
glossary.sentence: At every layer, we also add a bias input.

glossary.definition: The sum of squared differences between the actual and predicted outputs for all output neurons for all training examples. It is used to measure the performance of a neural network during learning.
glossary.sentence: The total error of the network is calculated as the sum of squared differences between the actual and predicted outputs for all output neurons for all training examples.

glossary.definition: A single instance in a dataset that contains input features and its corresponding real classification label (the real output) used during the learning process of a neural network.
glossary.sentence: a training example with its real classification label (the real output) is given to the perceptron;

glossary.definition: The process of changing the weights in a neural network based on the difference between actual and desired outputs. Adjustments are made only if there is a discrepancy.
glossary.sentence: if the predicted output is different from the real output, then the perceptron adjusts the weights; otherwise, if the predicted output is the same as the actual output, then the adjustments of the weights are not made because the performance of the perceptron network is considered to be satisfactory;

glossary.definition: A condition that determines when the learning process of a neural network should stop. It can be based on reaching a pre-defined maximum permissible error or completing a certain number of iterations (epochs).
glossary.sentence: The learning can be stopped if the total classification error on all training examples reaches the pre-defined maximum permissible error or the pre-defined number of iterations is achieved.

glossary.definition: The neurons in a neural network that perform computations. They calculate the weighted sum of inputs and apply an activation function to produce the output.
glossary.sentence: the number of processing units, the function for combining input signals, and the activation function to be used.

glossary.definition: The individual elements or variables that are used as inputs to a neural network. They are provided by the environment and fed into the input layer.
glossary.sentence: The input layer is pre-defined by the number of input features.

glossary.definition: The predefined classes or groups that a neural network must assign inputs to during a classification task. The number of these categories determines the number of processing units in the output layer.
glossary.sentence: the number of its processing units corresponds to the number of classification categories/classes.

glossary.definition: A system composed of interconnected processing units (neurons) that process information. It can be used for tasks such as pattern recognition and classification.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks have many hyperparameters: the number of layers, the number of processing units, the function for combining input signals, and the activation function to be used.

glossary.definition: A type of network architecture where information flows in one direction, from input to output through hidden layers. It does not have feedback connections.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, a feed-forward neural network has greater processing power, and it can model practically any function of arbitrary complexity, where the number of inputs and the number of hidden layers determine the function complexity (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: a method used to train multilayer perceptrons by modifying weights in all network layers through the propagation of error from the output layer to the input layer.
glossary.sentence: The learning in the multilayer perceptron is based on the backpropagation algorithm .

glossary.definition: a collection of rules used by the human experts that must be preserved in building a knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: The structure of rules used by the human experts must be preserved in building a knowledge base.

glossary.definition: a component of an expert system that uses rules to infer conclusions from given facts.
glossary.sentence: The selection of a search direction is based on the analysis of how real domain experts solve problems in a specific domain. If a domain expert first needs to gather some information and then tries to infer from it whatever can be inferred, choose the forward chaining inference engine.

glossary.definition: Artificial intelligence methods that do not use problem-specific knowledge, instead employing general techniques like search, logic, syntax-based computation, automatic reasoning.
glossary.sentence: In the first module of the study course, we considered two groups of artificial intelligence methods: weak methods, which do not use knowledge about a problem under consideration...

glossary.definition: Artificial intelligence methods based on extensive knowledge about the problem under consideration.
glossary.sentence: ...and strong methods, based on the great amount of knowledge about the problem under consideration.

glossary.definition: A specialist who understands methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge engineer is a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence...

glossary.definition: A person with deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a specific domain, who can provide domain-specific heuristic knowledge to solve problems more effectively.
glossary.sentence: Anyone can be considered a domain expert if he/she has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain...

glossary.definition: An automated system used by the domain expert or the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge, build a knowledge base, offer user interfaces for creating diagrams or structured descriptions of knowledge, and provide tools for analysis, generation, and consistency checking.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge acquisition system is an automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge...

glossary.definition: A knowledge-based intelligent system that solves problems in a narrowly specialised domain in a way and at a performance level similar to one of the human experts.
glossary.sentence: Intelligent computer systems that emulate the human expert’s methodology and performance are called expert systems...

glossary.definition: A set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge, which allows for creating a knowledge representation or description.
glossary.sentence: Knowledge representation schemas are means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge.

glossary.definition: A type of knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge in the form of logical statements using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and applies inference rules to derive new true knowledge from existing true knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Logical knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of logical statements...

glossary.definition: A type of knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a directed labelled graph, where nodes display simple objects/concepts and arcs represent relations between them.
glossary.sentence: Networked knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph...

glossary.definition: A type of knowledge representation schema that uses a graph to represent knowledge, with each node displayed as a complex structure containing all characteristics and components of an object/concept.
glossary.sentence: Structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge...

glossary.term: procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schema
glossary.definition: A type of knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.
glossary.sentence: Procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.

glossary.definition: Theories that propose knowledge on different concepts is related by associations in the human mind, defining the meaning of a concept in relation to other concepts.
glossary.sentence: Networked knowledge representation schemas are based on associationist theories of human mind...

glossary.definition: An experiment conducted by Collins and Quillian in 1969 to understand how people store knowledge, revealing that knowledge is stored at different hierarchical levels and requires time to navigate the hierarchical path from activated concepts to their features.
glossary.sentence: In 1969, Collins and Quillian made an experiment with aim of understanding how people store knowledge...

glossary.definition: A method of organizing knowledge where general features characterizing all birds are associated with a concept 'Bird', requiring navigation along the hierarchical path in memory to find specific answers.
glossary.sentence: The results of the experiment are displayed in Figure 3. So, the previously described experiment showed that...

glossary.definition: a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them
glossary.sentence: Collins and Quillian created a semantic network that is displayed in Figure 4.

glossary.definition: a relation represented by the title of 'is a' that allows showing hierarchical relations by relating narrower concepts to broader concepts
glossary.sentence: A relation 'subclass-class' (also 'subset-set', 'subtype-type', ' subkind -kind') is represented by a title of 'is a'

glossary.definition: a relation represented by the title of 'is an instance/example of' that allows creating hierarchical relations by relating one specific object in the problem domain to a class/set/type/kind it belongs to
glossary.sentence: A relation 'instance-class/set/type/kind' is represented by the title of 'is an instance/example of'

glossary.definition: a relation represented by the title of 'Part of' that allows relating parts of the object to the whole object
glossary.sentence: A relation 'part-whole' is represented by the title of 'Part of'

glossary.definition: a relation represented by the title of 'property/has a property of' that allows relating objects/concepts to their properties
glossary.sentence: A relation 'object-property' is represented by the title of 'property/has a property of'

glossary.definition: a relation represented by the title of 'value' that allows relating properties of objects/concepts to their values
glossary.sentence: A relation 'property-value' is represented by the title of 'value'

glossary.definition: relations that allow specifying any other kind of relations (for example, like, own, travel, etc.) that cannot be represented by the previously mentioned types of relations
glossary.sentence: Linguistic relations (Fig.11). They allow specifying any other kind of relations (for example, like, own, travel, etc.) that cannot be represented by the previously mentioned types of relations.

glossary.definition: a feature of semantic networks by which a narrower node automatically inherits knowledge from the network
glossary.sentence: Inheritance is a feature of semantic networks (Luger, 2009). Inheritance is a process by which a narrower node automatically inherits knowledge from the network

glossary.definition: use a graph to represent knowledge. However, comparing to the networked knowledge representation schemas, each node in the graph represents not a single concept/object, but a complex structure describing an object/concept together with its properties and parts
glossary.sentence: Structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge. However, comparing to the networked knowledge representation schemas, each node in the graph represents not a single concept/object, but a complex structure describing an object/concept together with its properties and parts.

glossary.definition: an example of structured knowledge representation schemas
glossary.sentence: Frames are an example of structured knowledge representation schemas.

glossary.definition: A procedure that is invoked as a side effect of some other action in the knowledge base (Luger, 2009). Demons are triggered automatically when a slot value is accessed (Mallach, 1994).
glossary.sentence: Demons control actions for slot-level manipulations (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: a type of expert system that uses production rules as a method for representing knowledge, with each rule having an IF part and a THEN part.
glossary.sentence: Rule-based expert systems have the following advantages: Production rules are a natural way of knowledge representation because human experts very often explain problem-solving using constructions like “if... then...” ( Negnevitsky , 2011 ).

glossary.definition: A component that stores the facts acquired from the user, conclusions derived in the reasoning process, confidence measures of conclusions, information coming from external programs, dead ends in the search process, and other information relevant to the case under consideration (Luger, 2009).
glossary.sentence: The working memory ensures this by storing the facts acquired from the user, conclusions derived in the reasoning process, confidence measures of conclusions, information coming from external programs, dead ends in the search process, and other information relevant to the case under consideration (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: A component that allows an expert system to explain its reasoning and justify the output ( Negnevitsky , 2011) because an expert system provides the user with a recommendation, plan, advice, conclusion, or any other result.
glossary.sentence: Explanation facilities are essential for any expert system because an expert system provides the user with a recommendation, plan, advice, conclusion, or any other result.

glossary.definition: A component that allows the user to interact with the system through a question-and-answer, menu-driven, natural language, or graphical interface (Luger, 2009).
glossary.sentence: The user interacts with the system through a user interface designed as a question-and-answer, menu-driven, natural language, or graphical interface (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: Additional components that can be added to the rule-based expert system.
glossary.sentence: However, additional components, such as external applications and developer interface, can be added as well.

glossary.definition: A component that allows the developer to edit rules in the knowledge base (add new rules as well as modify and delete existing rules) and to check the performance of the expert system after updating the rules ( Negnevitsky , 2011).
glossary.sentence: The developer interface is intended to edit rules in the knowledge base (add new rules as well as modify and delete existing rules) and to check the performance of the expert system after updating the rules ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A process that links the rules given in the knowledge base with the facts provided in the working memory (Negnevitsky, 2011).
glossary.sentence: The inference engine performs the control cycle 'Recognize-act' (Luger, 2009) to link the rules given in the knowledge base with the facts provided in the working memory (Negnevitsky, 2011).

glossary.definition: A description of the current state of the problem-solving process (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009) that is stored in the working memory.
glossary.sentence: The working memory contains a description of the current state of the problem-solving process (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009) that is called a pattern .

glossary.definition: Data that are separated from the knowledge base (Luger, 2009). These data are assumed to be correct when they are used (Hopgood, 2001).
glossary.sentence: The case-specific data are separated from the knowledge base (Luger, 2009). These data are assumed to be correct when they are used (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: Rules that the rule-based expert system uses to perform reasoning and reach a solution.
glossary.sentence: The inference engine carries out the reasoning by interpreting and applying the rules whereby the rule-based expert system reaches a solution (Hopgood, 2001; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: the set of enabled rules from which one rule is chosen to fire during conflict resolution.
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A technique for gathering information and then inferring from it whatever can be inferred ( Negnevitsky , 2011). It is typically used for problems of interpretation because, in this type of problems, most of the data are initially given, and the system searches for their interpretation (Luger, 2009; Hopgood, 2001).
glossary.sentence: Forward chaining is a technique for gathering information and then inferring from it whatever can be inferred ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A type of search in rule-based expert systems that starts with a goal (a hypothetical solution) and attempts to find the evidence to prove it.
glossary.sentence: In backward chaining , an expert system has the goal (a hypothetical solution), and the inference engine attempts to find the evidence to prove it ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A process that can demand significant computational resources, and the question is how to make the search more effective.
glossary.sentence: The search process can demand significant computational resources, and the question is how to make the search more effective.

glossary.definition: Three types of strategies used to make the search process in a rule-based expert system more effective (Luger, 2009): control through the selection of search direction; control through the selection of rule sequence and structure; control through the selection of conflict resolution strategy.
glossary.sentence: Three search control strategies are used for this purpose (Luger, 2009): control through the selection of search direction; control through the selection of rule sequence and structure; control through the selection of conflict resolution strategy.

glossary.definition: a type of inference engine used when a domain expert begins with a hypothetical solution and attempts to find facts to prove it.
glossary.sentence: However, if your domain expert begins with a hypothetical solution and then attempts to find facts to prove it, choose the backward chaining inference engine.

glossary.definition: a type of inference engine used when a domain expert first needs to gather some information and then tries to infer from it whatever can be inferred.
glossary.sentence: If a domain expert first needs to gather some information and then tries to infer from it whatever can be inferred, choose the forward chaining inference engine.

glossary.definition: a rule used in rule-based expert systems that has an IF part and a THEN part, where the IF part specifies conditions and the THEN part specifies actions or conclusions.
glossary.sentence: The structure and the order of production rules in the knowledge base determine how to search the knowledge base and how quickly it will be searched.

glossary.definition: a method of conflict resolution that prioritizes more specific rules based on the assumption that a specific rule processes more information than a general one ( Negnevitsky , 2011).
glossary.sentence: This method is also known as the longest matching strategy: it is based on the assumption that a specific rule processes more information than a general one ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: a method of conflict resolution where rules are assigned priority values, and the rule with the highest priority is chosen when multiple rules can fire.
glossary.sentence: to fire rules based on their priorities. Rules can be written so that each has an explicitly stated priority value, and, thus, if more than one rule can fire, the one with the highest priority is chosen (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: a method of conflict resolution where the rule that uses the data most recently entered in the working memory is fired first.
glossary.sentence: fire the rule that uses the data most recently entered in the working memory. This method relies on time tags attached to each fact in the working memory; thus, in the conflict set, the expert system first fires the rule whose antecedent uses the data most recently added to the working memory (Negnevitsky, 2011).

glossary.definition: a component of an expert system where facts are stored and accessed for rule application.
glossary.sentence: fire the rule that uses the data most recently entered in the working memory. This method relies on time tags attached to each fact in the working memory; thus, in the conflict set, the expert system first fires the rule whose antecedent uses the data most recently added to the working memory (Negnevitsky, 2011).

glossary.definition: a method used in production systems to choose one rule to fire from those that are able to fire.
glossary.sentence: Both the structure and the sequence of production rules are significant in the context of a chosen conflict resolution strategy.

glossary.definition: a statement in a rule-based expert system that has an IF part specifying conditions and a THEN part specifying actions or conclusions.
glossary.sentence: The structure of rules used by the human experts must be preserved in building a knowledge base.
